jokes.text: D'oh! [Screams]
jokes.setup: Opening sequence car crash scenario
jokes.punchline: Homer's signature catchphrase and scream
jokes.explanation: Iconic character moment that defines Homer - perfect economy and integration

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: D'oh!

jokes.text: Yabba dabba do!
jokes.setup: Opening sequence parody setup
jokes.punchline: Flintstones reference in opening credits
jokes.explanation: Standard pop culture reference, functional but not particularly creative

dialogue.speaker: Announcer
dialogue.line: The Simpsons!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: D'oh!

jokes.text: What do they do with these things after we seal 'em? I hear they dump 'em in an abandoned chalk mine and cover 'em with cement.
jokes.setup: Workers discussing nuclear waste disposal
jokes.punchline: Casually discussing environmental crimes
jokes.explanation: Good dark humor establishing the environmental theme

dialogue.speaker: Nuclear Plant Worker
dialogue.line: So we're just gonna dump all this nuclear waste in the river?

dialogue.speaker: Nuclear Plant Worker
dialogue.line: Sure, why not? The EPA won't find out.

dialogue.speaker: Nuclear Plant Worker
dialogue.line: Besides, it's not like the fish can complain.

jokes.text: I hear they're sending 'em to one of those southern states where the governor's a crook.
jokes.setup: Continuation of waste disposal discussion
jokes.punchline: Political corruption joke
jokes.explanation: Decent political satire but feels generic

jokes.text: Either way, I'm sleeping good tonight.
jokes.setup: After discussing environmental crimes
jokes.punchline: Worker's complete lack of concern
jokes.explanation: Perfect deadpan delivery caps the dark humor sequence

dialogue.speaker: Nuclear Plant Worker
dialogue.line: Environmental crimes? Hey, we're not committing crimes, we're committing... business!

jokes.text: Well, sir, where shall we dump this batch? The playground? No. All those bald children are arousing suspicion. To the park!
jokes.setup: Burns deciding where to dump nuclear waste
jokes.punchline: Bald children from radiation exposure
jokes.explanation: Brilliant dark humor - the casual mention of radiation causing baldness in children is perfectly Burns-like evil

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Smithers, where shall we dump the nuclear waste?

dialogue.speaker: Smithers
dialogue.line: How about the town reservoir, sir?

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Excellent idea, Smithers. The bald children will be a small price to pay for shareholder profits.

jokes.text: I think it's full, sir. That's ridiculous! The last tree held nine drums.
jokes.setup: Tree being used as waste storage
jokes.punchline: Burns's unrealistic expectations for tree capacity
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of the absurd environmental destruction

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Smithers, I want you to fill this tree with all of our industrial waste.

dialogue.speaker: Smithers
dialogue.line: But sir, a tree can only hold so much—

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: I don't want to hear your botanical excuses, Smithers. I want this tree filled to the brim with toxic sludge, nuclear byproducts, and whatever else we need to dispose of.

dialogue.speaker: Smithers
dialogue.line: Yes, sir. Though I should mention the tree is already showing signs of distress.

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Distress? It's a tree, Smithers. They're very sturdy. Now get to work before I use you as waste storage instead.

jokes.text: Mr. Burns, in light of your unbelievable contempt for human life... this court fines you three million dollars. Smithers, my wallet's in my right front pocket.
jokes.setup: Massive fine for environmental crimes
jokes.punchline: Burns treating it like pocket change
jokes.explanation: Perfect character moment - Burns's wealth makes legal consequences meaningless

dialogue.speaker: Judge
dialogue.line: Mr. Burns, you are fined $50 million for environmental crimes.

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Fifty million? That's more than I make in a year! Oh wait, no it isn't.

jokes.text: Oh, and, uh, I'll take that statue of Justice too. Sold!
jokes.setup: Burns already buying his way out of trouble
jokes.punchline: Literally purchasing Lady Justice
jokes.explanation: Brilliant metaphor for corrupted justice system made literal

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: How much for the statue?

dialogue.speaker: Judge
dialogue.line: That's Lady Justice, Mr. Burns. She's not for sale.

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: I'll give you one million dollars.

dialogue.speaker: Judge
dialogue.line: Sold!

jokes.text: Oh, Andy Capp. You wife-beating drunk. [Chuckles]
jokes.setup: Homer reading the comics
jokes.punchline: Casually describing Andy Capp's terrible behavior
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Homer's casual acceptance of problematic behavior

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Wow, Andy Capp is a drunk, a gambler, and he beats his wife. And yet, I can't stop reading about him!

jokes.text: Mmm, excellent. Hello, Lisa. I'm Genghis Khan. You'll go where I go... defile what I defile, eat who I eat.
jokes.setup: Educational VR history lesson
jokes.punchline: Disturbingly accurate historical portrayal
jokes.explanation: Clever subversion of sanitized education with historical reality

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Wow, this VR simulation is incredibly detailed and historically accurate.

dialogue.speaker: VR Genghis Khan
dialogue.line: I am Genghis Khan. I conquered vast empires and ruled with an iron fist.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: This is... disturbingly accurate. Maybe too accurate.

dialogue.speaker: VR Genghis Khan
dialogue.line: Education requires authenticity, young scholar.

jokes.text: Bart, we'll do anything you want. just call off your giant mechanical ants! Aah! [Laughing Evilly]
jokes.setup: Bart's fantasy of how to spend the money
jokes.punchline: Giant mechanical ants terrorizing Springfield
jokes.explanation: Perfect Bart fantasy - destructive, elaborate, and completely in character

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Giant mechanical ants terrorizing Springfield

jokes.text: Like a giant billboard that says, 'No fat chicks'? No!
jokes.setup: Discussing what the whole town can be proud of
jokes.punchline: Bart's inappropriate suggestion
jokes.explanation: Typical Bart inappropriate comment - fits character but not particularly clever

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: What is something the whole town can be proud of?

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Well, we're the only town in America with a giant donut.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: That's not something to be proud of, Bart.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: How about we're the only town where you can get a beer and a gun in the same store?

jokes.text: Could this town be any stupider? Order! Please rise for the, uh, Pledge of Allegiance. Get to the money!
jokes.setup: Lisa questioning town's intelligence
jokes.punchline: Immediate demonstration of town's impatience and greed
jokes.explanation: Good setup and immediate payoff showing town's single-minded focus

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: I can't believe how stupid this town is.

dialogue.speaker: Mayor Quimby
dialogue.line: Now, now, Lisa. I'm sure we're not that—

dialogue.speaker: Citizens
dialogue.line: Free money! Free money!

dialogue.speaker: Mayor Quimby
dialogue.line: Oh, never mind.

jokes.text: Don't you mean three million dollars? Of course. How silly of me.
jokes.setup: Quimby announces two million dollars
jokes.punchline: Corruption revealed through 'mistake'
jokes.explanation: Sharp political satire showing casual corruption and skimming

dialogue.speaker: Mayor Quimby
dialogue.line: I'm pleased to announce that the city has received a two million dollar grant!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Woo-hoo!

dialogue.speaker: Mayor Quimby
dialogue.line: However, due to a clerical error, it appears the money was meant for another town. So we'll have to return it.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: D'oh!

jokes.text: Excuse me. We could use the money to hire firemen... to finally put out that blaze on the east side of town. Boring!
jokes.setup: Reasonable suggestion to put out ongoing fire
jokes.punchline: Town finds actual emergency 'boring'
jokes.explanation: Great satire of public priorities - ongoing emergency less exciting than novelty

dialogue.speaker: Chief Wiggum
dialogue.line: We need to put out this fire!

dialogue.speaker: Citizen
dialogue.line: Good idea, Chief. That's very reasonable.

dialogue.speaker: Chief Wiggum
dialogue.line: Finally, someone agrees with me!

dialogue.speaker: Citizen
dialogue.line: Wait, is that it? No twist? No danger?

dialogue.speaker: Other Citizen
dialogue.line: This is boring. I thought there'd be more to this emergency.

dialogue.speaker: Crowd
dialogue.line: Yeah, yeah! Boring! We want excitement!

jokes.text: Hello. My name is Mr. Snrub... and I come from, uh, someplace far away.
jokes.setup: Burns in obvious disguise at town meeting
jokes.punchline: Terrible fake name and vague origin story
jokes.explanation: Iconic moment - 'Mr. Snrub' became a meme. Perfect example of Burns's arrogance and incompetence at deception

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Good evening. I am Monty Smithers, a local businessman of no particular importance.

dialogue.speaker: Lenny
dialogue.line: Hey, that's Mr. Burns!

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: No, I'm not. I'm Monty Smithers. I'm from... uh... Albany.

dialogue.speaker: Barney
dialogue.line: That's definitely Burns, man.

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Nonsense! I've never even heard of this Burns fellow. Now, if you'll excuse me, I have some... normal people activities to attend to.

jokes.text: Yes, that will do. Anyway... I-I say we invest that money back in the nuclear plant. I like the way Snrub thinks.
jokes.setup: Burns suggesting investing in his own plant
jokes.punchline: Smithers supporting the obvious fraud
jokes.explanation: Great escalation of the Snrub bit with Smithers's loyal participation in the farce

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Smithers, I've decided to invest all my money in my own plant. What do you think?

dialogue.speaker: Smithers
dialogue.line: Sir, that's a wonderful idea! Though I should mention, it's technically illegal.

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Illegal? Smithers, I'm 104 years old. What are they going to do, put me in jail?

dialogue.speaker: Smithers
dialogue.line: Well, sir, there is that possibility.

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Then it's settled. Start the paperwork immediately.

jokes.text: Pardon me, but I would like to see this money... spent on more police officers. I have been shot eight times this year... and as a result, I almost missed work. Crybaby.
jokes.setup: Wiggum requesting more police funding
jokes.punchline: Being shot 8 times only made him 'almost' miss work, citizen calls him crybaby
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist humor - the escalation from reasonable request to absurd dedication to calling him a crybaby is perfect

dialogue.speaker: Chief Wiggum
dialogue.line: Being shot 8 times only made me almost miss work.

dialogue.speaker: Citizen
dialogue.line: Crybaby!

jokes.text: My name is Marge Simpson, and I have an idea. It may sound a little boring at first. Chat a way. I'll just amuse myself with some pornographic playing cards.
jokes.setup: Marge presenting her reasonable idea
jokes.punchline: Quimby's immediate dismissal and inappropriate distraction
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Quimby's corruption and dismissiveness

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Mr. Mayor, I have a proposal that could really help our community.

dialogue.speaker: Mayor Quimby
dialogue.line: Yes, yes, that's all well and good, Mrs. Simpson, but have you seen the curves on that new intern in my office?

jokes.text: Whoo-hoo! Look at that pavement fly!
jokes.setup: Marge describing Main Street damage from heavy loads
jokes.punchline: Homer's excited reaction to destructive driving
jokes.explanation: Typical Homer moment - oblivious to consequences, excited by destruction

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: The heavy loads have destroyed Main Street! Look at all the damage!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Woohoo! Destructive driving!

jokes.text: Oh! I ain't fer it. I'm agin it!
jokes.setup: Town suddenly supporting Main Street repair
jokes.punchline: Grampa's contrarian nature regardless of the issue
jokes.explanation: Classic Grampa moment - automatic opposition without considering the issue

dialogue.speaker: Grampa Simpson
dialogue.line: Main Street needs repairs? Well, I'm against it!

dialogue.speaker: Homer Simpson
dialogue.line: But Dad, the whole town agrees we should fix it.

dialogue.speaker: Grampa Simpson
dialogue.line: That's exactly why I'm against it! If everyone's for something, it must be wrong!

jokes.text: You know, a town with money's a little like the mule with a spinning wheel. No one knows how he got it, and danged if he knows how to use it. [Laughter] Mule.
jokes.setup: Lanley's folksy wisdom about towns with money
jokes.punchline: Nonsensical analogy that the crowd finds profound
jokes.explanation: Brilliant con-man rhetoric - completely meaningless but delivered with confidence. The crowd's reaction to 'Mule' shows their susceptibility to manipulation

dialogue.speaker: Lyle Lanley
dialogue.line: Now, a town that sucks all the money out of its citizens with a bogus monorail scheme — that's a story worth telling.

dialogue.speaker: Lyle Lanley
dialogue.line: It's like a peanut on a stick — looks good from far, but far from good when it's close.

dialogue.speaker: Citizens
dialogue.line: *murmuring in agreement* Yeah... profound...

jokes.text: Probably the greatest-- Ah, it's not for you. It's more of a Shelbyville idea. Now wait just a minute. We're twice as smart as the people of Shelbyville. just tell us your idea, and we'll vote for it.
jokes.setup: Lanley pretending his idea isn't for Springfield
jokes.punchline: Town immediately commits to vote for unknown idea out of rivalry
jokes.explanation: Perfect con-man psychology - using rivalry to bypass rational decision-making

dialogue.speaker: Lyle Lanley
dialogue.line: Now, I don't want to say this idea is for Springfield, but what if I told you about a wonderful opportunity in a town just like yours?

dialogue.speaker: Mayor Quimby
dialogue.line: Well, we'd want to know more!

dialogue.speaker: Lyle Lanley
dialogue.line: I'm thinking of presenting it to Shelbyville first.

dialogue.speaker: Mayor Quimby
dialogue.line: Shelbyville?! No! We'll take it! Whatever it is, we'll vote yes immediately! We can't let those Shelbyville clowns get it first!

jokes.text: I've sold monorails to Brockway, Ogdenville and North Haverbrook... and by gum, it put them on the map!
jokes.setup: Lanley's sales pitch about successful monorails
jokes.punchline: Seemingly positive testimonial (later revealed as ominous)
jokes.explanation: Sets up the later reveal beautifully - 'put them on the map' becomes darkly ironic

dialogue.speaker: Lyle Lanley
dialogue.line: Well, sir, there's nothing on Earth like a genuine, bona fide, electrified, six-car monorail!

dialogue.speaker: Homer Simpson
dialogue.line: Monorail!

dialogue.speaker: Lyle Lanley
dialogue.line: Here's what I love about these things. It's so smooth. Why, on a monorail system, it would be impossible to be vocally distracting!

dialogue.speaker: Barney Gumble
dialogue.line: Is there a chance the track could bend?

dialogue.speaker: Lyle Lanley
dialogue.line: Not on your life, my Hindu friend!

dialogue.speaker: Apu Nahasapeemapetilon
dialogue.line: What about us brain-dead slobs?

dialogue.speaker: Lyle Lanley
dialogue.line: You'll be given cushy jobs!

dialogue.speaker: Grampa Simpson
dialogue.line: Were you sent here by the Devil?

dialogue.speaker: Lyle Lanley
dialogue.line: No, good sir, I'm on the level.

dialogue.speaker: Marge Simpson
dialogue.line: The ring came off my pudding can.

dialogue.speaker: Lyle Lanley
dialogue.line: Take my pen knife, my good man. I swear it's the best darn knife there is.

jokes.text: Well, sir, there's nothing on earth like a genuine, bona fide... electrified, six-car monorail. What'd I say? Monorail! What's it called? Monorail! That's right, monorail!
jokes.setup: Lanley's sales pitch building momentum
jokes.punchline: Crowd getting caught up in chanting the word
jokes.explanation: One of the most iconic Simpsons moments - perfect satire of mob mentality and sales manipulation

dialogue.speaker: Lyle Lanley
dialogue.line: Now, who here wants to take a ride on the Lemon Tree of Desire down the Candy Cane Forest Express way through my chocolate implementation factory?

dialogue.speaker: Citizens
dialogue.line: Monorail! Monorail! Monorail!

dialogue.speaker: Lyle Lanley
dialogue.line: I said lemon tree!

dialogue.speaker: Citizens
dialogue.line: Monorail! Monorail! Monorail!

jokes.text: I hear those things are awfully loud. It glides as softly as a cloud. Is there a chance the track could bend? Not on your life, my Hindu friend.
jokes.setup: Citizen's legitimate concerns about monorail
jokes.punchline: Lanley's rhyming dismissals
jokes.explanation: Brilliant musical sequence parody - addresses real concerns with meaningless but catchy responses

dialogue.speaker: Citizen
dialogue.line: But what about safety? What about—

dialogue.speaker: Lyle Lanley
dialogue.line: Is there a chance the track could bend?

dialogue.speaker: Citizen
dialogue.line: Not on your life, my Hindu friend!

dialogue.speaker: Citizen
dialogue.line: But Main Street's still all cracked and broken—

dialogue.speaker: Lyle Lanley
dialogue.line: Sorry, Mom, the line's been spoken: "What about us brain-dead slobs?"

dialogue.speaker: Citizen
dialogue.line: You'll be given cushy jobs!

dialogue.speaker: Citizen
dialogue.line: Were you sent here by the Devil?

dialogue.speaker: Lyle Lanley
dialogue.line: No, good sir, I'm on the level.

jokes.text: What about us brain-dead slobs? You'll be given cushy jobs. Were you sent here by the devil? No, good sir. I'm on the level.
jokes.setup: Citizens asking pointed questions
jokes.punchline: Lanley's rhyming non-answers
jokes.explanation: Continues the brilliant musical con - 'brain-dead slobs' is particularly self-aware

dialogue.speaker: Citizens
dialogue.line: Is there a chance the track could bend?

dialogue.speaker: Lyle Lanley
dialogue.line: Not on your life, my Hindu friend!

dialogue.speaker: Citizens
dialogue.line: What about us brain-dead slobs?

dialogue.speaker: Lyle Lanley
dialogue.line: You'll be given cushy jobs!

dialogue.speaker: Citizens
dialogue.line: Were you sent here by the devil?

dialogue.speaker: Lyle Lanley
dialogue.line: No, good sir, I'm on the level!

dialogue.speaker: Citizens
dialogue.line: The ring came off my pudding can.

dialogue.speaker: Lyle Lanley
dialogue.line: Take my pen knife, my good man. I swear it's not a scam.

jokes.text: The ring came off my pudding can! Take my pen knife, my good man. I swear it's Springfield's only choice. Throw up your hands and raise your voice.
jokes.setup: Grampa's non-sequitur about pudding can
jokes.punchline: Lanley incorporating random interruption into his song
jokes.explanation: Perfect absurdist moment - Grampa's random interjection seamlessly incorporated into the manipulation

dialogue.speaker: Grampa Simpson
dialogue.line: Back in my day, we'd eat pudding from a can, and we were happy!

dialogue.speaker: Lyle Lanley
dialogue.line: Monorail! It puts all your tax dollars to good use. Back in my day, we'd eat pudding from a can, and we were happy! Monorail!

jokes.text: But Main Street's still all cracked and broken. Sorry, Mom, the mob has spoken.
jokes.setup: Marge's final reasonable objection
jokes.punchline: Bart dismissing logic with rhyme
jokes.explanation: Perfect capper to the sequence - reason defeated by catchy rhythm

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: But Bart, that's a terrible idea. You'll break your neck!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: I'm gonna do it anyway, Mom. Eat my shorts and logic!

jokes.text: Mono-- D'oh!
jokes.setup: Homer caught up in monorail chanting
jokes.punchline: Trips or stumbles mid-chant
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer physical comedy interrupting the mob mentality

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Monorail! Monorail! Monorail! Monorail!... *trips* ...Ow!

jokes.text: Well, you should have written a song like that guy.
jokes.setup: Marge's reasonable concerns ignored for flashy presentation
jokes.punchline: Acknowledging the power of the musical number
jokes.explanation: Smart meta-commentary on how presentation trumps substance

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: But what about the structural integrity and—

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Shh! A musical number!

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Oh.

jokes.text: Miss Hoover? That is hard to believe. Oh, you. [Chuckles]
jokes.setup: Miss Hoover introduces herself
jokes.punchline: Lanley's inappropriate comment about her appearance
jokes.explanation: Mild character humor showing Lanley's sleazy nature

dialogue.speaker: Miss Hoover
dialogue.line: Hello, I'm Miss Hoover, Springfield Elementary's teacher.

dialogue.speaker: Lyle Lanley
dialogue.line: Well, hello there, Miss Hoover. You know, in all my years as a monorail salesman, I've never seen such a... radiant complexion.

jokes.text: Can it outrun The Flash? You bet. Can Superman outrun The Flash? Uh, sure. Why not?
jokes.setup: Children asking superhero-related questions
jokes.punchline: Lanley giving impossible answers without thinking
jokes.explanation: Good con-man moment - agreeing to anything without considering contradictions

dialogue.speaker: Student
dialogue.line: Can Superman fly backwards?

dialogue.speaker: Lyle Lanley
dialogue.line: Sure, why not?

dialogue.speaker: Student
dialogue.line: Can he see through lead?

dialogue.speaker: Lyle Lanley
dialogue.line: Absolutely!

dialogue.speaker: Student
dialogue.line: Can he hear a pin drop from a mile away?

dialogue.speaker: Lyle Lanley
dialogue.line: You betcha!

jokes.text: I'd like you to explain... why we should build a mass transit system... in a small town with a centralized population. [Chuckles] Young lady... that's the most intelligent question I've ever been asked. Really? Oh, I could give you an answer. But the only ones who'd understand it would be you and me... and that includes your teacher.
jokes.setup: Lisa asks the one smart question
jokes.punchline: Lanley flatters her intelligence while avoiding the answer
jokes.explanation: Brilliant con-man psychology - deflecting the smart question with flattery and condescension

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: But what about the shoddy construction and the fact that it'll probably derail?

dialogue.speaker: Lyle Lanley
dialogue.line: Well, ma'am, you raise an excellent point. You're obviously a young woman of considerable intelligence. Have you ever considered a career in monorail design?

jokes.text: Next question. You there, eating the paste.
jokes.setup: Lanley moving on from Lisa's difficult question
jokes.punchline: Calling on the paste-eating kid
jokes.explanation: Good transition showing Lanley preferring easier questions

dialogue.speaker: Lyle Lanley
dialogue.line: Well, I think we've exhausted this line of questioning. Now, who here likes donuts?

dialogue.speaker: Ralph Wiggum
dialogue.line: Ooh! Ooh! Me! Me!

dialogue.speaker: Lyle Lanley
dialogue.line: Excellent. And you are?

dialogue.speaker: Ralph Wiggum
dialogue.line: Ralph Wiggum.

dialogue.speaker: Lyle Lanley
dialogue.line: Well, Ralph, how would you like to eat all the donuts you want on a train that never stops?

jokes.text: Are you stuck in a dead-end job? Maybe. Are you squandering the precious gift Of life in front of the idiot box? What's it to you? Are you on your third beer of the evening? Does whiskey count as beer?
jokes.setup: Commercial asking probing lifestyle questions
jokes.punchline: Homer's increasingly defensive and revealing answers
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer moment - the escalation from defensive to revealing is spot-on

dialogue.speaker: TV Commercial
dialogue.line: Do you ever feel like you're not living up to your potential?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: No.

dialogue.speaker: TV Commercial
dialogue.line: Do you wish you had more energy?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: No.

dialogue.speaker: TV Commercial
dialogue.line: Do you feel isolated and alone?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Yes. Wait, no. No!

dialogue.speaker: TV Commercial
dialogue.line: Do you ever think about killing yourself?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Regularly.

jokes.text: Your lifelong dream was to run out on the field during a baseball game... and you did it last year, remember?
jokes.setup: Homer claiming monorail conducting is his lifelong dream
jokes.punchline: Marge revealing his actual fulfilled lifelong dream
jokes.explanation: Great character moment showing Homer's constantly shifting 'lifelong dreams'

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Conducting the monorail has been my lifelong dream!

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, your lifelong dream was to eat the world's largest hoagie.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Oh yeah... I forgot about that. Well, after I conduct the monorail, that'll be my next dream.

jokes.text: Before we begin, is anyone here an investigative reporter? I am, and she is. Well, I'd like you to please leave. Should we take our hidden camera? Would you?
jokes.setup: Lanley asking about investigative reporters
jokes.punchline: Politely asking them to leave, which they do
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist moment - incompetent journalism meets polite criminal

dialogue.speaker: Lyle Lanley
dialogue.line: Now, if you'll excuse me, I have a luncheon appointment with the President of Paraguay.

dialogue.speaker: Reporters
dialogue.line: We'll be in touch.

jokes.text: True or false: You can get mono from riding the monorail. Mmm, false. No, wait. Maybe it's true. No, you were right. It's false.
jokes.setup: Training question about mononucleosis
jokes.punchline: Homer's confusion about the wordplay
jokes.explanation: Typical Homer confusion moment - overthinking a simple pun

dialogue.speaker: Instructor
dialogue.line: What is mononucleosis commonly called?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Is it the kissing disease?

dialogue.speaker: Instructor
dialogue.line: Yes, that's correct.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Woo-hoo! I'm a doctor!

jokes.text: You know, I used to think you were stuck... in an emasculating, go-nowhere job. [Chuckles] Kids. But now, I wanna follow in your footsteps. Do you want to change your name to Homer Junior? The kids can call you Hoju.
jokes.setup: Bart's brutal assessment of Homer's job
jokes.punchline: Homer's pathetic eagerness for Bart's approval
jokes.explanation: Great father-son dynamic - Homer's desperate need for respect from his son

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Dad, you're a safety inspector at a nuclear power plant, yet you fall asleep at your desk every day. You're basically useless.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Oh man, am I really that bad?

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Yeah, you're terrible.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Well, as long as you think I'm cool, that's what matters.

jokes.text: According to this book, the monorail goes over 1 50 miles an hour. What if something goes wrong? What if? What if I'm taking a shower and I slip on a bar of soap? Oh, my God, I'd be killed!
jokes.setup: Marge's legitimate safety concern
jokes.punchline: Homer's escalation to shower death scenario
jokes.explanation: Good Homer logic - derailing valid concern with absurd parallel

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, you can't keep leaving your underwear all over the bathroom floor. Someone could slip and get hurt.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Oh Marge, what are the odds of that happening? One in a million?

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: It only takes once, Homer.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Well, if I pick up my underwear, I'll be late getting to the shower. Then I'll slip on the tile and crack my head open. Then I'll be in a coma for twenty years and wake up talking like Cher.

jokes.text: We monorail conductors are a crazy breed... half in love with death, gobbling up danger like ordinary men eat peanuts.
jokes.setup: Homer trying to sound romantic and dangerous
jokes.punchline: Absurd overstatement about monorail conducting
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer delusion - turning mundane job into heroic fantasy with overwrought language

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I'm like a dangerous man... a man who conducts monorails.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I've caused more deaths than a thousand serial killers combined.

jokes.text: So then, 'mono' means 'one,' and 'rail' means 'rail.' And that concludes our intensive three-week course.
jokes.setup: Three-week monorail training course
jokes.punchline: Entire education consists of defining the word
jokes.explanation: Brilliant satire of inadequate training - three weeks reduced to basic etymology

dialogue.speaker: Instructor
dialogue.line: Welcome to the three-week monorail training course. Your entire education consists of defining the word 'monorail.'

jokes.text: Homer, there's a family Of possums in here. I call the big one Bitey.
jokes.setup: Marge discovering possums in the monorail
jokes.punchline: Homer's casual naming of dangerous wildlife
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer moment - accepting dangerous situations with childlike naming. 'Bitey' became iconic.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer! There are possums in the monorail!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: So? We have possums at home. I call that one Bitey.

jokes.text: How much did you see? Uh, nothing incriminating. Good. Well, bye.
jokes.setup: Marge catching Lanley in suspicious activity
jokes.punchline: Lanley asking the wrong question, Marge's honest answer
jokes.explanation: Good criminal incompetence moment - accidentally revealing guilt

dialogue.speaker: Lyle Lanley
dialogue.line: You didn't see me, right?

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: I'm afraid I did.

jokes.text: Go away! There ain't no monorail, and there never was!
jokes.setup: Marge investigating previous monorail towns
jokes.punchline: Desperate denial from traumatized survivor
jokes.explanation: Good dramatic revelation - PTSD-like denial from the con's previous victims

dialogue.speaker: North Haverbrook Citizen
dialogue.line: Monorail? We don't know nothin' about no monorail!

jokes.text: And the celebrity on the maiden voyage was Gallagher.
jokes.setup: Kobb explaining the previous monorail disaster
jokes.punchline: Gallagher as the pathetic celebrity guest
jokes.explanation: Good period-specific humor - Gallagher as bottom-tier celebrity

dialogue.speaker: Sebastian Cobb
dialogue.line: Well, as you know, we've had our share of problems with the monorail system.

dialogue.speaker: Gallagher
dialogue.line: Yeah, I heard about that. Say, you got any watermelons around here?

jokes.text: I locked my keys in there. Get a rock.
jokes.setup: After delivering dire warning about the monorail
jokes.punchline: Mundane car trouble requiring primitive solution
jokes.explanation: Decent anticlimax after dramatic warning - bathos humor

dialogue.speaker: Sebastian Cobb
dialogue.line: This monorail will be a nightmare of danger and destruction!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: But what about our car?

dialogue.speaker: Sebastian Cobb
dialogue.line: Your car is fine.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Oh good.

dialogue.speaker: Lenny
dialogue.line: Hey Homer, your car's got a flat tire.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Well, I'll just fix it myself. How hard can it be?

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Dad, you need a jack.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I'll use a brick.

jokes.text: Krusty, why won't you answer my calls? You've never even seen our son!
jokes.setup: Krusty at celebrity monorail opening
jokes.punchline: Woman confronting him about abandoning their child
jokes.explanation: Good character background humor - Krusty's personal failures at public event

dialogue.speaker: Woman
dialogue.line: Krusty! You abandoned our child!

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: Kid, I got a casino to run and a show to do. I ain't got time for no responsibilities!

dialogue.speaker: Woman
dialogue.line: That's our son!

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: Yeah, yeah, that's nice. Now beat it, I got celebrities to schmooze.

jokes.text: He's cool, he's sexy, he's 34 years old.
jokes.setup: Introducing teen heartthrob from TV show
jokes.punchline: Revealing his actual inappropriate age
jokes.explanation: Good satire of Hollywood's fake teen actors - prescient of later scandals

dialogue.speaker: Announcer
dialogue.line: Ladies and gentlemen, please welcome teen heartthrob sensation!

dialogue.speaker: Announcer
dialogue.line: He's only 47 years old!

jokes.text: What ya been up to, Lurleen? I spent last night in a ditch.
jokes.setup: Celebrity interview about recent activities
jokes.punchline: Brutally honest admission of rock bottom
jokes.explanation: Good dark humor about celebrity addiction struggles

dialogue.speaker: Announcer
dialogue.line: So Lurleen, what have you been up to lately?

dialogue.speaker: Lurleen Lumpkin
dialogue.line: Well, I hit rock bottom. I'm livin' in my car, sellin' my hair for food money, and my only friend is a possum named Mr. Whiskers.

jokes.text: I'd say this vessel could do at least warp five. And let me say... 'May the force be with you.' Do you even know who I am? I think I do. Weren't you one Of the Little Rascals?
jokes.setup: Nimoy making Star Trek references, mixing franchises
jokes.punchline: Homer's complete cultural ignorance
jokes.explanation: Good pop culture humor - nerd reference meeting complete ignorance

dialogue.speaker: Leonard Nimoy
dialogue.line: Live long and prosper.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Is that a Star Wars thing?

dialogue.speaker: Leonard Nimoy
dialogue.line: No, it's from Star Trek.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Oh, same thing.

jokes.text: Little lady, I'd love to, but I have to catch a plane. The ride only takes a minute. Yeah, well, my plane leaves in less than one minute.
jokes.setup: Child asking why Lanley won't ride his own monorail
jokes.punchline: Absurd timing excuse for avoiding his own product
jokes.explanation: Good con-man exit strategy - obvious avoidance with weak excuse

dialogue.speaker: Child
dialogue.line: Mr. Lanley, why won't you ride your monorail?

dialogue.speaker: Lyle Lanley
dialogue.line: Oh, well, I'd love to, but I'm afraid I have a terrible fear of heights. Also, I'm very busy that day.

jokes.text: We're too late! I shouldn't have stopped for that haircut. Sorry.
jokes.setup: Marge arriving too late to stop the monorail
jokes.punchline: Blaming trivial delay during crisis
jokes.explanation: Typical Marge moment - polite concern even during disaster

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Wait! Stop the monorail! Homer's on it!

dialogue.speaker: Conductor
dialogue.line: Sorry ma'am, we're already moving. Nothing can stop this monorail now.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: But you don't understand - my husband is in terrible danger!

dialogue.speaker: Conductor
dialogue.line: Ma'am, I'm going to have to ask you to take your seat or exit the station.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: This is no time for procedure! A human life is at stake!

dialogue.speaker: Conductor
dialogue.line: Rules are rules, ma'am. Now please, you're holding up the line.

jokes.text: Actually, you see, the doors on Star Trek were not mechanical. We had a stagehand on either side... who would pull the door open when he saw you approach.
jokes.setup: Casual conversation during monorail ride
jokes.punchline: Behind-the-scenes revelation about famous sci-fi effects
jokes.explanation: Nice meta-humor about TV production, though not particularly strong

dialogue.speaker: Leonard Nimoy
dialogue.line: You know, people always ask me about the special effects on Star Trek.

dialogue.speaker: Leonard Nimoy
dialogue.line: Well, I'll let you in on a secret - we used to just shake the camera.

dialogue.speaker: Leonard Nimoy
dialogue.line: That's it. For the entire series.

jokes.text: The lever you have pulled--brakes-- Is not in service. Please make a note of it.
jokes.setup: Homer trying to stop runaway monorail
jokes.punchline: Bureaucratic automated message during crisis
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist moment - deadly emergency met with customer service politeness

dialogue.speaker: Computerized Voice
dialogue.line: Please stay in your seats. A complimentary beverage will be served shortly.

jokes.text: Well, judging by your husband's cowardly scream... 180 miles an hour.
jokes.setup: Marge asking about the monorail's speed
jokes.punchline: Nimoy's logical but insulting assessment method
jokes.explanation: Good Spock-like logic applied to Homer's cowardice

dialogue.speaker: Marge Simpson
dialogue.line: How fast does it go?

dialogue.speaker: Leonard Nimoy
dialogue.line: Well, to reach the necessary speed, we'll need to throw another chair on the fire.

jokes.text: Ah. It's making me dizzy. I'm gonna take a nap.
jokes.setup: Monorail spinning out of control
jokes.punchline: Nimoy's casual response to mortal danger
jokes.explanation: Good deadpan reaction to crisis - very Spock-like composure

dialogue.speaker: Leonard Nimoy
dialogue.line: I'm sorry, I can't. I'm unable to maintain the speed necessary to stay on the track.

jokes.text: All right, I'm in charge here. Oh, run along, Quimby. I think they're dedicating a phone booth somewhere. Watch it, you talking tub of donut batter.
jokes.setup: Power struggle during emergency
jokes.punchline: Fat Tony's creative insult for Quimby
jokes.explanation: 'Talking tub of donut batter' is a memorably creative insult

dialogue.speaker: Fat Tony
dialogue.line: Listen here, Quimby. You're nothin' but a two-bit politician with the brains of a pork chop and the spine of a jellyfish.

dialogue.speaker: Mayor Quimby
dialogue.line: How dare you speak to me that way! I am the democratically elected leader of this city!

dialogue.speaker: Fat Tony
dialogue.line: Democratically elected? The only thing you're good at democratic-ing is democrating yourself right out of a job, pal.

jokes.text: Hey, I got pictures of you, Quimby. You don't scare me. That could be anyone's ass.
jokes.setup: Fat Tony threatening blackmail
jokes.punchline: Quimby's confident defense of his anonymity
jokes.explanation: Good corruption humor - Quimby's oddly specific confidence

dialogue.speaker: Fat Tony
dialogue.line: Listen here, Quimby. I got pictures of you with that... let's just say 'lady of the evening.' You're gonna pay up, or these photos go public.

dialogue.speaker: Mayor Quimby
dialogue.line: Fat Tony, my good man, I'm not worried. Half the people in this town don't even know what I look like. The other half are too drunk to remember.

jokes.text: Hey, according to the charter... as chief constable I'm supposed to get a pig every month... 'and two comely lasses of virtue true.' Keep the pig. How many broads do I get?
jokes.setup: Emergency meeting devolving into charter reading
jokes.punchline: Wiggum's priorities during crisis
jokes.explanation: Perfect Wiggum moment - missing the point spectacularly during crisis

dialogue.speaker: Chief Wiggum
dialogue.line: We need to focus on what's important here.

dialogue.speaker: Mayor Quimby
dialogue.line: Chief, what are you talking about? We have a crisis!

dialogue.speaker: Chief Wiggum
dialogue.line: According to section 12, paragraph B of the Springfield Police Charter, I'm entitled to a 20-minute donut break every four hours.

jokes.text: Wait a minute. We can just shut off the power! No such luck. It's solar-powered. Solar power. When will people learn?
jokes.setup: Attempt to stop monorail by cutting power
jokes.punchline: Homer blaming renewable energy for the crisis
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Homer logic - blaming environmentally friendly technology for the disaster it couldn't cause

dialogue.speaker: Chief Wiggum
dialogue.line: Cut the power! Cut the power!

dialogue.speaker: Technician
dialogue.line: We can't, Chief! The monorail runs on renewable energy!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Renewable energy? Why do we even bother with this green stuff?

jokes.text: A solar eclipse. The cosmic ballet goes on. Does anyone want to switch seats?
jokes.setup: Solar eclipse conveniently stopping the solar-powered monorail
jokes.punchline: Nimoy's poetic observation and mundane question
jokes.explanation: Good contrast between poetic and practical - 'cosmic ballet' became quotable

dialogue.speaker: Leonard Nimoy
dialogue.line: A solar eclipse. The cosmic ballet of the heavens.

dialogue.speaker: Leonard Nimoy
dialogue.line: But where will I get my dinner reservations?

jokes.text: North Haverbrook. Where have I heard that name before? Oh, no. Oh, no! There he is-- seat 3-F!
jokes.setup: Lanley's plane making emergency landing
jokes.punchline: Landing in town he previously conned, mob justice
jokes.explanation: Good karmic justice - con man facing consequences in previous victim town

dialogue.speaker: Lyle Lanley
dialogue.line: Ladies and gentlemen, we've had a small emergency landing here in North Haverbrook.

dialogue.speaker: North Haverbrook Citizen
dialogue.line: Wait a minute... Lyle Lanley!

dialogue.speaker: Lyle Lanley
dialogue.line: Now, now, I'm sure we can work this out...

dialogue.speaker: North Haverbrook Citizens
dialogue.line: Get him! String him up! He sold us that defective monorail!

jokes.text: Krusty wants out! No. The world needs laughter.
jokes.setup: Krusty panicking during monorail crisis
jokes.punchline: Nimoy's philosophical response to Krusty's cowardice
jokes.explanation: Good character moment - Nimoy being unexpectedly profound about Krusty's importance

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: We're all gonna die! We're all gonna die!

dialogue.speaker: Leonard Nimoy
dialogue.line: Calm down. What is the worst that could happen?

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: We could die!

dialogue.speaker: Leonard Nimoy
dialogue.line: Yes, and...?

jokes.text: Homer, there's a man here who thinks he can help you. Batman? No, he's a scientist. Batman's a scientist. It's not Batman!
jokes.setup: Marge offering help during crisis
jokes.punchline: Homer's Batman fixation and defense
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer logic - defending Batman's scientific credentials during crisis

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, I'm here to help you through this crisis.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Crisis? Marge, I'm Batman now. Batman doesn't need help.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, you're not Batman. You're just wearing a cape.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Exactly. Batman wears a cape. Therefore, I am Batman. It's science, Marge.

jokes.text: Arr! Ya call that an anchor?
jokes.setup: Homer using makeshift anchor to stop monorail
jokes.punchline: Sea Captain's professional criticism
jokes.explanation: Good character-specific humor - Sea Captain's nautical expertise

dialogue.speaker: Sea Captain
dialogue.line: Arrr, that be the worst anchor I ever seen. Ye need to use a proper nautical anchor, not some piece of junk. The weight distribution be all wrong, and ye'll be capsizin' in no time flat!

jokes.text: Arr! Ya call that an anchor?
jokes.setup: Homer using makeshift anchor to stop monorail
jokes.punchline: Sea Captain's professional critique during crisis
jokes.explanation: Good character moment - Sea Captain's expertise showing up randomly. Perfect timing and character voice.

dialogue.speaker: Sea Captain
dialogue.line: Ye gads, man! That's not how ye anchor a vessel! Ye need proper ballast and a certified marine-grade anchor, not some... whatever landlubber contraption that be!

jokes.text: Donuts. Is there anything they can't do? Dad, you're a hero. Yes, son. I'm the best mono-thingy guy there ever was.
jokes.setup: Homer successfully stopping the monorail with donuts
jokes.punchline: Homer's philosophical appreciation and garbled terminology
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer moment - finding profound meaning in junk food and mangling professional terminology

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: You know, I've learned that life is all about the simple things... a donut, a beer, and a son who respects his old man.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Dad, you just stopped a monorail with donuts.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Mmm... donuts. Yes, yes, I have achieved a state of... uh... donuttaineousness. It's like nirvana, but with more glaze.

jokes.text: Well, my work is done here. What do you mean, Your work is done? You didn't do anything. Didn't I?
jokes.setup: Nimoy claiming his work is done after the crisis
jokes.punchline: Bart calling out his non-contribution, Nimoy's mysterious response
jokes.explanation: Good celebrity cameo ending - mysterious departure after doing nothing

dialogue.speaker: Leonard Nimoy
dialogue.line: My work here is done.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: But you didn't do anything!

dialogue.speaker: Leonard Nimoy
dialogue.line: Yes, but I did it... in space.

jokes.text: And that was the only folly... the people of Springfield ever embarked upon... except for the Popsicle stick skyscraper... and the 50-foot magnifying glass... and that escalator to nowhere.
jokes.setup: Marge claiming the monorail was Springfield's only folly
jokes.punchline: Immediately contradicting herself with multiple other follies
jokes.explanation: Brilliant escalating contradiction - each new folly more absurd than the last

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: The monorail was Springfield's only folly.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: What about the lemon tree?

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Well, besides that.

dialogue.speaker: Barney
dialogue.line: The plow king business?

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Okay, besides that.

dialogue.speaker: Lenny
dialogue.line: The sky bridge to the mall?

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Well, I... Look, it was our only major folly!

jokes.text: Whoa! Aah!
jokes.setup: People using the escalator to nowhere
jokes.punchline: Inevitable falling and screaming
jokes.explanation: Simple visual payoff to the escalator to nowhere concept

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Whoa, whoa, whoa!

dialogue.speaker: Citizen 1
dialogue.line: Ahhhhh!

dialogue.speaker: Citizen 2
dialogue.line: Help!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: We're falling!

dialogue.speaker: Multiple Citizens
dialogue.line: Ahhhhhhhhh!
